content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
June 17, 1997
Kathleen Vienna
211 “F” Avenue
Coronado, California 92118
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-97-258
Dear Ms. Vienna:
This letter is a response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’
QUESTION
Is a Parks and Recreation Commissioner disqualified from participating in a decision to
modify the pool use fee charged to the Peninsula Water Polo Club and other youth organizations,
when the Commissioner’s spouse is the Water Polo Club’s President and the person bringing the
request?
CONCLUSION
When a public official makes a decision personally affecting the public official or a
member of his or her immediate family, the official must disqualify himself or herself, unless the
decision will not affect the official’s economic interests by an amount equal to or in excess of
$250 in 12 months.
FACTS
Coronado’s City Council created the Parks and Recreation Commission, a five-member
board. The Commission is authorized to investigate, consider, and recommend to the city
council, suggestions to promote recreational activities for the people ofthe city. You are one of
the commissioners.
Government Code sections 81000
- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
428 3 STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No. A-97-258
Page 2
The city currently charges a fee to groups, including the Peninsula Water Polo club
(“club”), for use ofthe city pool. It appears that the club passes the city pool use fee costs, as
part of the membership fee, on to the club members. The president of the club is Kevin Vienna,
your spouse. His position with the club is uncompensated.
In June 1996 and March 1997, Kevin Vienna presented a proposal for reductions in the
fees charged to community related youth groups for use ofthe city swimming pool. The pending
decision is currently tabled, awaiting review by the Fair Political Practices Commission on
whether you may participate.
You and your spouse also have a child who is currently a member of the club. His
membership fees for participation in the club are paid from family income. The child is eligible
to participate in the club for the next 16 months.
ANALYSIS
Economic Interests
Section 87100 ofthe Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. Among other economic interests, section 87103
specifies that a public official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally, on the official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:
“(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans by
a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business
on terms available to the public without regard to official status,
aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by or promised to the public official within
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.” (Section
87103(c).)
According to the information you have provided, your child is a member of the club and
your spouse is the president of the club. If the pool use fees were to be changed, as your spouse
has requested, the liability ofyour family income would be reduced by $65 per year. Therefore,
you may have a potentially disqualifying economic interest in the outcome of the decision. To
determine if you have to disqualify yourself from a decision, we must first determine ifyou have
a disqualifying interest under section 87103, then determine whether the decision will have a
foreseeable and material financial effect on those interests.


File No. A-97-258
Page 3
Family Members
The Act contemplates that a public official may be disqualified when the official or a
member of his or her family may be impacted by a decision. (Section 87103.) “Immediate
family” includes a public official’s spouse and dependent children. (Section 82029.) The
reduction in pooi fees for your son will affect you or a member of your immediate family
personally. Therefore, you may not participate in such decisions if they will have a reasonably
foreseeable material effect on you.
Source ofIncome
When a public official’s spouse receives compensation in the form of wages, salary, etc.,
the wages that the spouse receives will be traced back to the entity or source ofthe income.
(Howard Advice Letter, No. A-95-361, Aishire Advice Letter, No. A-95-352, No. A-95-352;
Herring Advice Letter, No. A-96-162; Hackard Advice Letter, No. A-84-070.) A source of
income is potentially disqualifying to the public official because a public official has a
community property interest in the income ofthe spouse. (Section 82030.) In this case, the
potentially disqualifying source of income to you would be the Coronado Water Polo Club.
However, you have stated that your spouse does not receive any compensation from the
Coronado Water Polo Club. Therefore, the Club would not be a disqualifying source of income
ofyours.
2
Foreseeablility
As previously stated, a public official is prohibited from participating in a decision that
will have a foreseeable fmancial effect on his or her economic interests. An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not
required. However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In re
Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198; Hawkins Advice Letter, A-95-026.)
The decision to grant or decline your spouse’s request for a reduction in the pool use fees
will determine whether a $65 a year savings is realized by you and your family. The potential for
such savings makes it reasonably foreseeable that your decision will have a financial effect on
your economic interests.
Materiality
Family Members
2 You have provided no facts indicating that the club would be a potentially disqualifying economic
interest under section 87103 (a),(b),(d), or (e).


File No. A-97-258
Page 4
A decision will be material if it will result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or
liabilities of the official or his or her immediate fa.mily increasing or decreasing by at least $250
in any 12-month period. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(4).) According to the facts you provided, your
son is a member ofthe club. Thus, the maximum savings your family income would receive by
passage of your spouse’s request would be $65 a year. Because this amount is not equal to or in
excess of $250 within the past 12 months, the effect of the decision will not be deemed to be
material under regulation 18702.1 (a)(4).
3
The Public Generally Exception
If at a later time the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of your governmental
decisions become material, disqualification would only be required if the effect on your fmancial
interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. (Section 87103.) Therefore,
for the “public generally” exception to apply, a decision must affect your financial interests in
substantially the same manner as it would affect a significant segment of the public. (Regulation
18703.) A substantial segment of the public is affected if at least 10 percent of the population in
the jurisdiction of the official’s agency would be affected. (Regulation 18703(a)(1)(A)(i).)
In the absence of specific information, this factual determination will be left to your
discretion. (In re Ogeisby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
SGC:DW:ak
The Commission has only determined that a public official is not legally required to disqualify himself or
herself from a decision that will not have a material fmancial effect (i.e., an amount not equal to or exceeding $250).
A public official may still voluntarily disqualify himself or herselfto avoid the image of impropriety.
Legal Division

QUESTION: Is a Parks and Recreation Commissioner disqualified from participating in a decision to
modify the pool use fee charged to the Peninsula Water Polo Club and other youth organizations,
when the Commissioner’s spouse is the Water Polo Club’s President and the person bringing the
request?

CONCLUSION: When a public official makes a decision personally affecting the public official or a
member of his or her immediate family, the official must disqualify himself or herself, unless the
decision will not affect the official’s economic interests by an amount equal to or in excess of
$250 in 12 months.
